name: Yamunacharya
dates_notes: Sectarian chronicle dating only; long lifespan in tradition is suspect.
core_thesis: Yāmuna is the first Śrīvaiṣṇava to give Viśiṣṭādvaita the form of an argued philosophical system rather than a liturgical-exegetical synthesis. His position is that Brahman — Nārāyaṇa-with-Śrī — is a *qualified* unity whose body (*śarīra*) consists of all conscious selves (*cit*) and all non-conscious matter (*acit*), each really distinct yet inseparable (*apṛthak-siddha*) from him as their inner controller (*antaryāmin*). Against the early *māyāvāda* he argues that the *jīva* is a substantial, atomic, eternally self-aware *jñātṛ*; against Buddhist *vijñāna-vāda* and against an undifferentiated-*cit* monism he defends *dharmi-bhūta-jñāna* — awareness as a self-revelatory substance bearing real attributes — and *yathārtha-khyāti*, the veridicality of all cognition (error being only partial apprehension, never the cognising of a non-existent). His specific innovations: a rigorous *Saṃvit-Siddhi* epistemology of self-luminous awareness; a Pāñcarātra-defence (*Āgama-Prāmāṇya*) integrating non-Vedic Vaiṣṇava revelation with *śruti-prāmāṇya*; and the elevation of *prapatti* — surrender to Śrī-Nārāyaṇa — to a recognised *upāya* alongside *bhakti-yoga*.

engaged_works.title: Siddhi-Trayam
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored triad of Siddhi-treatises (Ātma-, Īśvara-, Saṃvit-Siddhi).
engaged_works.summary: A triad of *prakaraṇa*-treatises — *Ātma-Siddhi*, *Īśvara-Siddhi*, *Saṃvit-Siddhi* — establishing in turn the reality of the individual self, of a personal *Īśvara*, and of self-luminous awareness. The *Ātma-Siddhi* defends the *jīva* as an atomic, eternal, knowing substance (*jñātṛ*), refuting Buddhist no-self doctrines and the Advaitic identification of *ātman* with undifferentiated *cit*: the self is essentially knower, not knowing-as-such. The *Īśvara-Siddhi* mounts a theistic *anumāna* for a personal omniscient creator, taking the Naiyāyika design-argument and rebuilding it on Vedic foundations against Mīmāṃsaka objections. The *Saṃvit-Siddhi*, the foundational Viśiṣṭādvaita epistemology, defends *dharmi-bhūta-jñāna* — awareness as itself a substance bearing attributes, self-revelatory (*svayam-prakāśa*) yet directed always to a content — against Buddhist *vijñāna-vāda* and against proto-Advaita opponents who would collapse the knower-known distinction into pure unqualified consciousness. The triad is the philosophical bedrock on which Rāmānuja's *Śrī-Bhāṣya* stands.

engaged_works.title: Agama-Pramanya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored defense of Pāñcarātra agama-validity.
engaged_works.summary: Polemical *prakaraṇa* defending the scriptural authority (*prāmāṇya*) of the Pāñcarātra *saṃhitā*-corpus against Mīmāṃsaka and Smārta objections that, being non-Vedic and authored by Bhagavān himself, the Pāñcarātra cannot count as *śruti* and its initiates fall outside the four *varṇa*-orders. Yāmuna's strategy is multi-pronged: (i) the Pāñcarātra is the direct utterance of Nārāyaṇa, who is the *vedasya kartā*, and so its authority is no weaker than that of the Veda; (ii) its prescriptions do not contradict *śruti* but specify what *śruti* leaves undetermined regarding Vaiṣṇava liturgy; (iii) Pāñcarātra-initiates (*bhāgavata*-s) are not a fifth *varṇa* but qualified *brāhmaṇa*-s under a particular *kalpa*. The work matters historically because it secured the legitimacy of the Śrīvaiṣṇava temple-cultus and theology as orthodox Vaidika, and philosophically because it formalises a layered theory of *prāmāṇya* that Rāmānuja and Vedānta Deśika later extend.

engaged_works.title: Stotra-Ratna
engaged_works.summary: Sixty-five-verse Sanskrit hymn to Nārāyaṇa, the literary template for the entire Śrīvaiṣṇava *stotra*-tradition. Beneath its devotional surface the *Stotra-Ratna* enacts the doctrinal architecture of the *Siddhi-Trayam*: it addresses Nārāyaṇa as Brahman *saviśeṣa* (qualified by infinite auspicious attributes — *ananta-kalyāṇa-guṇa*), as the inner-controller (*antaryāmin*) whose body is the totality of *cit* and *acit*, and as the sole refuge to whom *prapatti* (utter self-surrender) is the natural response of the *ākiñcana* (one stripped of all other resource). Verses such as 22 (*na dharma-niṣṭho 'smi*) and 47–53 give the canonical Śrīvaiṣṇava articulation of *śaraṇāgati* — the surrendering self as agent only of recognising its own incapacity. The work matters because it shows that Yāmuna's philosophical *prakaraṇa-s* and his liturgical hymnody express one and the same position: *bhakti* is not subordinate to *jñāna* but is *jñāna* in its mature, relational form, terminating in surrender to the divine person.

engaged_works.title: Catuh-Shloki
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored four-verse hymn to Śrī.
engaged_works.summary: Four-verse Sanskrit hymn to Śrī (Lakṣmī), foundational for Śrīvaiṣṇava *Śrī-tattva* — the doctrine of the goddess as eternal, inseparable consort and co-mediatrix (*puruṣakāra*) of Nārāyaṇa. The four verses establish in compressed form: (i) Śrī's eternity and ontological non-difference yet personal distinction from Nārāyaṇa; (ii) her status as the universal mother (*jaganmātṛ*) whose mercy (*kṛpā*) is the operative principle of grace; (iii) her role as *puruṣakāra* — the one who solicits the Lord's compassion on behalf of the surrendered *jīva*; (iv) the surrendering self's place at her feet as the proper attitude of *prapatti*. The text is doctrinally load-bearing because the later Vaḍakalai-Tenkalai split is partly a quarrel over how to read it — whether Śrī is herself an *upāya* (Tenkalai) or only the mediating advocate enabling Nārāyaṇa's grace (Vaḍakalai). Both schools acknowledge the *Catuḥ-Ślokī* as canonical; their divergence is exegetical, not textual.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
dehendriyamanaḥprāṇadhībhyo 'nyo 'nanyasādhanaḥ /
nityo vyāpī pratikṣetram ātmā bhinnaḥ svataḥ sukhī //
key_passages.text_critical_note: The transmitted GRETIL text reads `ananyasādhanaḥ`. The attached commentary later rejects an older printed reading `hānasvabhāva` and explains `ananyasādhanaḥ` as self-manifestness (`ananyena svena sādhanaṃ siddhiḥ prakāśaḥ yasya`).

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: dehebhyaḥ indriyebhyaḥ manasaḥ prāṇebhyaḥ dhībhyāś ca
samasa_vigrahas.note: The verse compresses the non-self series into one ablative dvandva.
Under the commentary's explanation, this marks the self as not needing a second revealer.

karaka_structure.note: Subject of the implied copula.
The self is distinguished away from this non-self aggregate.
Distributive locus: each bodily field.

verb_modality.note: Nominal verse with an implied present copula.

key_passages.english_close: The self, distinct from body, senses, mind, vital breaths, and cognitions, is self-manifest, eternal, pervasive, distinct in each bodily field, and by nature blissful.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This is not just a slogan but Yāmuna's own compact thesis-verse for the self: it fixes the self against body-mind reduction, against momentariness, and against pure-consciousness monism in one place. It fairly represents the Ātma-Siddhi because the surrounding commentary unpacks exactly these predicates as the work's programmatic claims.
key_passages.source_edition: Atma Siddhi, Yamuna, GRETIL plaintext (materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/yamuna_atma_siddhi_gretil.txt; corpus_meta.tsv).

like a country with one king

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: cetanaṃ cācetanaṃ cātmā yasya tasya bhāvaḥ
samasa_vigrahas.note: The world's constitution as a conscious-nonconscious complex.

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: ekaḥ rājā yasmin deśe saḥ iva
samasa_vigrahas.note: A polity structured by one ruler.

karaka_structure.note: Explicit subject of `kurvate`.
Instrumental of the one presiding agent.
Object performed.
Each inferential clause predicates a property of the world.

verb_modality.note: Implied copula in the nominal predications.

key_passages.english_close: That world is perceptible because it is an object of knowledge, because it is an entity, like a pot and the like. This world follows one will because it is non-conscious, like one's own body. All conscious beings, being directed by one, perform action because their agency depends on relation to a body, like skin and the rest. The disputed world has one principal person, because it consists of conscious and non-conscious factors, like a land with one king.
key_passages.why_this_passage: These verses are useful precisely because they are not merely a devotional assertion: they condense Yāmuna's inferential strategy for a personal Lord who orders both conscious and non-conscious reality. That makes them a fair representative of the Īśvara-Siddhi's project of arguing toward a real, governing Īśvara rather than simply presupposing him.
key_passages.source_edition: Isvara Siddhi, Yamuna, GRETIL plaintext (materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/yamuna_isvara_siddhi_gretil.txt; corpus_meta.tsv).

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
tasmāt prapañcasadbhāvo nādvaitaśrutibādhitaḥ //
svapramāṇabalāt siddhaḥ śrutyā cāpy anumoditaḥ //
tenādvitīyaṃ brahmeti śruter artho 'yam ucyate //
dvitīyagaṇanāyogyo nāsīd asti bhaviṣyati //
samo vābhyadiko vāsya yo dvitīyas tu gaṇyate //

pada_analysis.gloss: not fit for counting as a second

samasa_vigrahas.note: The real being of the world-manifold.
Negated by the relevant scriptural sentence.
From the force of the world's own valid means of knowing.
Unfit to enter a count as a co-equal second.

karaka_structure.note: Subject of the first nominal predication.
Ground by which world-reality is established.
Instrument of corroboration.
Subject of `ucyate`.
Genitive relation: the meaning belonging to the scriptural statement.
Relative subject in the final definition.
Reference back to Brahman.

verb_modality.note: Past time member of the triad.
Present time member of the triad.
Future time member of the triad.

key_passages.english_close: Therefore the real existence of the world-manifold is not negated by the non-duality scripture: it is established by the force of its own means of knowledge, and is also approved by scripture. Thus the meaning of the scriptural statement 'Brahman is without a second' is said to be this: there was not, is not, and will not be anything countable as a second to it, whether equal to it or superior to it.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: without a second in the sense of no equal or superior counterpart

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is one of the clearest places where Yāmuna states that Upaniṣadic non-duality does not cancel the world's reality but only denies any equal or superior second to Brahman. It fairly represents his position because it is not an isolated flourish: it explicitly ties ontology to the force of ordinary and scriptural pramāṇas, which is exactly how the Saṃvit-Siddhi argues.
key_passages.source_edition: Samvit Siddhi, Yamuna, GRETIL plaintext based on Roque Mesquita's 1988 edition (materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/yamuna_samvit_siddhi_gretil.txt; corpus_meta.tsv).

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
na dharmaniṣṭho 'smi, na cātmavedī, na bhaktimāṃs tvaccaraṇāravinde |
akiñcano 'nanyagatiḥ śaraṇya ! tvatpādamūlaṃ śaraṇaṃ prapadye ||
na ninditaṃ karma tad asti loke sahasraśo yan na mayā vyadhāyi |
so 'haṃ vipākāvasare mukunda ! krandāmi sampraty agatis tavāgre ||
nimajjato 'nanta ! bhavārṇavāntaś cirāya me kūlam ivāsi labdhaḥ |
tvayāpi labdhaṃ bhagavann idānīm anuttamaṃ pātram idaṃ dayāyāḥ ||

pada_analysis.morphology: acc sg neut past passive participle

pada_analysis.gloss: in the depth of the ocean of becoming

nom sg neut past passive participle

samasa_vigrahas.note: The base of the Lord's feet as refuge.
At the time of karmic fruition.
Inside the ocean of transmigratory becoming.

karaka_structure.note: Subject of `asmi`, `prapadye`, and `krandāmi`.
Locus for the denied possession of bhakti.
Object approached in `prapadye`.
Temporal locus for the cry of helplessness.
Vocatives intensifying dependence on the Lord.
Genitive relation: the speaker as recipient-vessel of compassion.

verb_modality.note: Identification of the Lord as the discovered shore.

key_passages.english_close: I am not one steadfast in dharma, nor a knower of the self, nor one possessed of devotion at your lotus-feet. Possessing nothing, with no other refuge, O refuge-giver, I take refuge in the root of your feet. There is no blameworthy deed in the world which I have not performed thousands of times. I, at the time of karmic fruition, O Mukunda, now cry out before you, bereft of recourse. For me, long sinking in the depth of the ocean of becoming, O Endless One, you are like a shore at last found; and by you too, O Lord, this unsurpassed vessel of compassion has now been found.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: refuge, the act and state of resorting
I take refuge; the verbal core of prapatti

key_passages.why_this_passage: Verse 22 is famous, but taking 22-24 together prevents cherry-picking: the surrender is embedded in confessed moral failure, karmic vulnerability, and the Lord's compassionate initiative. That is exactly how Yāmuna presents prapatti in the Stotra-Ratna, so this selection fairly represents the work rather than isolating a slogan.
key_passages.source_edition: Stotra Ratna, Yamuna, GRETIL plaintext (materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/yamuna_stotra_ratna_gretil.txt; corpus_meta.tsv).
